claims.claim: The half-life refresh gate reduces stale-assumption uses by 59.3% and wrong planning choices by 51.8% relative to a cached-assumption baseline, with positive net utility at the default refresh cost.

claims.claim: A sensitivity sweep across 15 cost and TTL-scale configurations reveals a clear boundary: net utility remains positive up to a refresh cost of 0.5 (in the simulation's cost units) across all TTL scales, but becomes mixed or negative at refresh cost 1.0, even though error reduction remains substantial at that cost.

claims.claim: The Half-Life Planning mechanism addresses this by assigning each piece of evidence a half-life reflecting its expected rate of change, computing a time-to-live (TTL) from that half-life, and requiring refresh before any expired fact can be used in a planning decision.

evidence_refs.quote: Long-running autonomous agents risk accumulating stale assumptions as the facts underlying their plans drift over time. We propose Half-Life Planning, a governance mechanism in which every task, memory, and assumption carries a time-to-live derived from its volatility half-life; expired items must be refreshed before they can influence planning decisions. We evaluate the mechanism in a deterministic toy-model simulation of a long-running planner operating over 64 facts with heterogeneous drift r
